events.text: The patient is a 55-year-old female named Diane Castellano. She has a history of intermittent sun exposure during outdoor recreational activities, no personal history of prior melanoma, and no family history of melanoma or other cancers. She is a non-smoker with no significant comorbidities at the time of diagnosis. She works as a landscape architect and lives in the southwestern United States.

At age 55 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) MELANOMA, MALIGNANT (ICD-O-3 histology code 8720/3) of the SKIN OF THE BACK (ICD-O-3 site code C44.5). The primary lesion was a 2.8 cm ulcerated nodular melanoma on the left mid-back, Clark Level V, Breslow thickness 6.1 mm, with mitotic rate 8/mm2. Tumor was ulcerated. The patient presented with synchronous metastases including multiple subcutaneous nodules on the trunk and extremities (cutaneous/in-transit), bilateral inguinal and axillary lymphadenopathy, and multiple hepatic lesions on staging imaging. LDH at diagnosis was elevated at 487 U/L (normal <225 U/L). ECOG performance status 1. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Summary Stage: DISTANT. Tumor Size: 28 mm.

At age 55 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from wide local excision of the left back primary lesion and sentinel lymph node biopsy. The primary tumor demonstrated nodular melanoma, ulcerated, Breslow depth 6.1 mm, Clark Level V, mitotic rate 8/mm2, with extensive lymphovascular invasion. Sentinel lymph node biopsy of the left axillary basin demonstrated metastatic melanoma involving 3 of 4 sentinel nodes with extracapsular extension. Immunohistochemistry: S100 positive, SOX10 positive, HMB-45 positive, Melan-A positive, Ki-67 approximately 55%. BRAF V600E immunohistochemistry: strongly positive (VE1 clone). PD-L1 expression by 22C3 assay: CPS 5 (low positive).

At age 55 years, the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging scan), which showed cancer. Metastatic sites identified: multiple bilateral pulmonary micronodules (largest 6 mm, indeterminate but concerning), bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy with the largest right inguinal node measuring 2.4 cm in short axis, multiple left axillary lymph nodes enlarged up to 3.1 cm, at least 8 hepatic lesions distributed throughout both lobes ranging from 0.8 to 3.7 cm, and at least 4 subcutaneous nodules on the trunk (largest 1.8 cm on right flank). No brain metastases identified on contrast-enhanced MRI brain obtained concurrently. No bone lesions on staging bone scan.

At age 55 years, the patient had an MRI brain with and without contrast (staging), which showed no cancer. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal brain parenchyma.

At age 55 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for metastatic melanoma based on a specimen obtained from the primary cutaneous lesion (left back excision). A comprehensive 500-gene tumor panel with matched germline sequencing was performed. Actionable alterations identified: BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%, tier I actionable), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss), CDKN2B homozygous deletion (copy number loss). Additional somatic alterations: TP53 p.R248W missense mutation (VAF 41%), PTEN p.R130* nonsense mutation (VAF 22%, partial loss), RAC1 p.P29S mutation (VAF 12%), MAP2K1 (MEK1) amplification (copy number gain 6x), TERT promoter c.-124C>T mutation (VAF 55%), RB1 loss of heterozygosity. No NRAS mutation detected (rules out concurrent NRAS/BRAF). No NF1 mutation. No KIT mutation. No GNAQ/GNA11 mutation. No ALK, ROS1, or NTRK fusions detected by RNA-based fusion analysis. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 18.4 mutations/Mb (high). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 by NGS tumor proportion: 8%. Germline analysis revealed no pathogenic variants in CDKN2A germline, BRCA1/2, or other hereditary cancer genes.

At age 55 years, the patient was initiated on dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily orally combined with trametinib 2 mg once daily orally as first-line targeted therapy for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma. Intent: palliative/disease control. Treatment rationale: rapid disease control given elevated LDH, high burden of hepatic metastases, and symptomatic subcutaneous disease. The patient was counseled on common side effects including pyrexia, rash, photosensitivity, and hyperglycemia.

At age 55 years (4 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with marked response to therapy. The patient reported significant improvement in her palpable subcutaneous nodules, which had visibly flattened. She noted mild fatigue and one episode of low-grade fever (38.1 C) that self-resolved. Repeat LDH obtained: 198 U/L (normalized). Physical exam: subcutaneous nodules on trunk markedly reduced, inguinal lymphadenopathy reduced bilaterally. ECOG performance status improved to 0. No dose modifications required. Continuing current regimen.

At age 55 years (6 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed a grade 2 pyrexia (temperature 39.2 C) consistent with dabrafenib-associated drug fever. She was managed with a temporary 3-day hold of both dabrafenib and trametinib, antipyretics, and oral hydration. Fever resolved within 48 hours of drug hold. Both agents were resumed at reduced dose (dabrafenib 100 mg twice daily, trametinib 1.5 mg once daily) per protocol. Subsequently tolerated without recurrence of fever. CTCAE grade 2.

At age 55 years (3 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with response. Interval improvement compared to baseline: hepatic lesions significantly reduced, previously seen 8 lesions now with the largest measuring 1.1 cm (down from 3.7 cm), representing approximately 70% reduction in hepatic tumor burden. Inguinal and axillary lymphadenopathy markedly reduced. Subcutaneous nodules no longer visualized on CT. Pulmonary micronodules stable and felt to be benign. Response assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE per RECIST 1.1. LDH: 145 U/L (normal). The patient reported excellent quality of life.

At age 55 years (3 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with response to therapy. Discussed impressive partial response on dabrafenib/trametinib with normalized LDH. Counseled the patient about the expected median duration of response (approximately 9-12 months) and the importance of planning for future immunotherapy. The dose of dabrafenib was escalated back to 150 mg twice daily as tolerated. ECOG performance status 0. Continuing targeted therapy and planning restaging in 3 months.

At age 55 years (6 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with response, though slightly less pronounced than prior. Hepatic lesions stable to minimally enlarged compared to 3-month scan: largest lesion now 1.4 cm (previously 1.1 cm). Slight increase in a right inguinal lymph node (1.1 cm vs 0.8 cm previously). Subcutaneous disease not visualized. Assessment: near-complete to partial response, with early hint of stabilization rather than continued regression. LDH: 189 U/L (still normal). Decision made to continue therapy with close monitoring.

At age 55 years (8 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging) along with MRI brain with contrast, which showed cancer with progression. Compared to prior scans: definite progression of hepatic disease with at least 3 new hepatic lesions and increase in known lesions (largest now 4.2 cm). New right axillary lymphadenopathy. New left inguinal node enlargement 2.8 cm. Two new small subcutaneous nodules on the right thigh. MRI brain: two new small brain metastases identified, right frontal lobe (0.8 cm) and left cerebellar hemisphere (0.6 cm), both without surrounding edema or mass effect. Assessment: PROGRESSIVE DISEASE per RECIST 1.1, acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition, with new CNS involvement. LDH elevated at 412 U/L.

At age 55 years (8 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had an urgent oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with progression. Discussed confirmation of acquired resistance to dabrafenib/trametinib. New CNS metastases identified. Multidisciplinary tumor board discussion completed. Plan: (1) stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to two brain metastases before initiating immunotherapy, (2) discontinue dabrafenib/trametinib, (3) initiate ipilimumab/nivolumab combination immunotherapy after SRS. Patient counseled extensively on risks of combination immunotherapy including immune-related adverse events. ECOG performance status 1. Patient agreeable to plan.

At age 55 years (8.5 months from initial diagnosis), the patient underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to two brain metastases. Right frontal lobe lesion (0.8 cm): treated with single fraction 20 Gy. Left cerebellar lesion (0.6 cm): treated with single fraction 20 Gy. Treatment delivered using a CyberKnife system with frameless stereotactic technique. Both lesions treated in a single session. The patient tolerated the procedure well with no acute neurologic complications. Post-SRS MRI at 1 month demonstrated decreased enhancement in both treated lesions consistent with treatment effect.

At age 55 years (approximately 9 months from diagnosis, 3 weeks after SRS completion), the patient was initiated on ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks combined with nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (combination immunotherapy, 4 doses planned) for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma with acquired resistance to targeted therapy and prior SRS for CNS metastases. Intent: palliative with potential for durable response. The patient was counseled extensively on immune-related adverse events (irAEs), particularly colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, and endocrinopathies.

At age 55 years (after cycle 1 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer. After first cycle, the patient noted mild fatigue and a pruritic skin rash on the upper trunk (grade 1), managed with topical triamcinolone cream. No diarrhea. LFTs within normal limits. Thyroid function normal. No dose modifications. Proceeding with cycle 2 as scheduled. ECOG performance status 1.

At age 55 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 6 weeks into immunotherapy), the patient developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. She presented to the emergency department with 10-12 loose/watery bowel movements per day, abdominal cramping, and blood-tinged stool. She was afebrile. Stool infectious workup negative (C. difficile, bacterial cultures, ova and parasites negative). CT abdomen/pelvis demonstrated diffuse colonic wall thickening and mucosal enhancement consistent with immune-mediated colitis. Flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed severe mucosal inflammation with erosions and ulcerations. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held. The patient was admitted and initiated on methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day IV. CTCAE grade 3. This represented a serious adverse event requiring hospitalization.

At age 55 years (during hospitalization for immune-mediated colitis), the patient had an inpatient oncologist assessment, which showed cancer. After 5 days of IV methylprednisolone, the patient had improvement to grade 1 diarrhea (3-4 stools/day). She was transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day. Gastroenterology co-management ongoing. Infliximab was not required given adequate steroid response. Discharged home after 7-day hospitalization with oral prednisone taper plan over 8 weeks. Given the grade 3 toxicity, ipilimumab was permanently discontinued per protocol. Nivolumab may be cautiously resumed after complete resolution of colitis and steroid taper to <10 mg/day prednisone equivalent.

At age 55 years (approximately 11 months from diagnosis, restaging after 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab and during steroid taper), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with mixed/early response. Hepatic lesions: slight decrease in some lesions, stable others; largest hepatic lesion now 3.4 cm (previously 4.2 cm at progression). Lymphadenopathy slightly improved. Subcutaneous nodules on right thigh no longer visualized. MRI brain: previously treated right frontal and left cerebellar lesions demonstrate decreased enhancement and reduced size consistent with treatment response to SRS. No new brain lesions. LDH: 298 U/L. Assessment: early response, mixed, with some features of continued activity. Plan: resume nivolumab monotherapy maintenance after steroid taper complete.

At age 55 years (approximately 12 months from diagnosis, after completion of prednisone taper to below 10 mg/day equivalent), the patient was initiated on nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as single-agent maintenance immunotherapy, following permanent discontinuation of ipilimumab due to prior grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. Intent: maintenance immunotherapy for metastatic melanoma. Patient counseled on signs and symptoms of colitis recurrence and other immune-related adverse events. GI precautions discussed. Patient instructed to contact the clinic immediately for any change in bowel habits.

At age 55 years (after cycle 2 of nivolumab maintenance, approximately 13 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with response. Patient tolerating nivolumab maintenance well. No recurrence of gastrointestinal symptoms. Mild fatigue, grade 1. No new rash. Thyroid function: TSH elevated at 8.2 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0), free T4 low-normal at 0.8 ng/dL, consistent with grade 1 immune-mediated hypothyroidism. Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. ECOG performance status 1. Improving energy. Continuing nivolumab maintenance.

At age 55 years (13 months from diagnosis), the patient developed grade 1 immune-mediated hypothyroidism, likely related to nivolumab therapy. TSH 8.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL. Symptoms included fatigue and mild cold intolerance. Managed with levothyroxine replacement therapy (50 mcg daily, titrated to 75 mcg daily at follow-up). Thyroid function normalized within 6 weeks of initiation of replacement therapy. No dose modification of nivolumab required for grade 1 endocrinopathy. CTCAE grade 1.

At age 55 years (approximately 15 months from diagnosis, after 3 cycles of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with response. Continued improvement compared to prior imaging: hepatic lesions decreased substantially with the largest now measuring 1.8 cm (previously 3.4 cm). Multiple smaller hepatic lesions no longer visualized. Lymphadenopathy resolved bilaterally. No new subcutaneous lesions. Pulmonary micronodules stable and benign in appearance. MRI brain concurrently obtained: no new brain lesions; previously treated lesions demonstrate continued treatment effect/post-SRS changes without viable tumor. LDH: 167 U/L (normal). Assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE per RECIST 1.1, ongoing deepening response on nivolumab maintenance.

At age 56 years (approximately 20 months from diagnosis, after 8 cycles of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with continued deep partial response. Hepatic lesions: 3 residual lesions with the largest now measuring 0.9 cm (previously 1.8 cm). Other lesions barely perceptible or resolved. No lymphadenopathy. No subcutaneous disease. MRI brain: stable post-treatment changes in right frontal and left cerebellar regions, no viable intracranial tumor, no new lesions. LDH: 152 U/L (normal). Assessment: DEEP PARTIAL RESPONSE, ongoing durable response on nivolumab maintenance. The patient has done exceptionally well.

At age 56 years (approximately 20 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer responding to therapy. The patient is experiencing a durable deep partial response on nivolumab maintenance. She is tolerating therapy well with well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No evidence of colitis recurrence. No new immune-related adverse events. Performance status excellent at ECOG 0. Quality of life subjectively very good; she has returned to full-time landscape architecture work. Discussed potential for treatment-free interval consideration after 2 years of total immunotherapy duration per emerging clinical evidence. Plans for continued nivolumab maintenance with reassessment at 24-month mark. LDH normal. Thyroid function stable.

At age 56 years (approximately 24 months from diagnosis, after completing planned 2-year immunotherapy duration mark), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with continued response. Hepatic lesions: 2 residual tiny hypodense lesions measuring 0.4 and 0.5 cm, at the limit of resolution, possibly residual scar tissue versus minimal residual disease. No new hepatic lesions. No lymphadenopathy. No extrahepatic disease. MRI brain: stable, no viable intracranial tumor. LDH: 148 U/L (normal). Assessment: Near-complete response / very deep partial response. Multidisciplinary discussion regarding treatment pause after 2 years of immunotherapy therapy.

At age 56 years (24 months from diagnosis, multidisciplinary tumor board discussion), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed near-complete response to therapy with no clinical evidence of active disease. Discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Given: (1) 2 years of total immunotherapy, (2) deep near-complete response, (3) residual hepatic lesions at limit of resolution possibly representing scar/treated disease, (4) patient preference to explore treatment pause, the decision was made to hold nivolumab after the current cycle and pursue observation with close surveillance imaging every 3 months. Patient counseled on signs of recurrence and the importance of reporting new symptoms. She was agreeable and emotionally relieved. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine. ECOG performance status 0. Plan: surveillance CT and MRI brain every 3 months for first year off therapy.

documents.text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit type: Established patient, office visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
Routine 3-month restaging visit. Patient with stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E+ melanoma, currently on Tafinlar/Mekinist (Tafinlar 150 mg BID / tram 2 mg daily). Here to review recent CT imaging and discuss ongoing management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Castellano is a 55-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of stage IV nodular melanoma (primary of the back, Breslow 6.1 mm, ulcerated, BRAF V600E+, 3/4 SLN positive at time of original WLE/SLNB). She presented initially with hepatic metastases (up to 3.7 cm, 8 discrete lesions), bilateral inguinal and axillary lymphadenopathy, and subcutaneous nodules. Brain MRI at staging was negative. LDH was elevated at baseline. NGS showed BRAF V600E as the dominant driver with co-alterations including CDKN2A/2B deletion, TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, RAC1 mutation, MAP2K1 amplification, and TERT promoter; TMB 18.4 mut/Mb.

She was started on Tafinlar/Mekinist (dab/tram) approximately 3 months ago as first-line systemic therapy given the BRAF V600E alteration and high disease burden. At her 4-week visit she had already shown a very encouraging clinical response -- subcutaneous nodules had flattened appreciably and LDH had normalized. At approximately 6 weeks she developed grade 2 pyrexia (fevers to 38.8-39.2C), which was managed with a brief dose hold and then dose reduction per standard protocol; she was subsequently retitrated and has been tolerating the full dose of dab 150 mg BID without further temperature issues for the past several weeks.

Today she reports overall feeling quite well. She has returned to part-time landscape architecture work. No new lumps or bumps noticed under the skin. No headaches, visual changes, focal neurological symptoms. No abdominal pain, jaundice, or right upper quadrant discomfort. Appetite is good. Energy is good, maybe 90% of her baseline.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: No fever currently. Mild fatigue, improved from prior. No significant weight loss (she notes she has actually regained about 3 lbs she had lost at diagnosis).
- HEENT: No headaches. No visual disturbance.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough. The pulmonary micronodules seen on staging CT are under surveillance.
- GI: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. No abdominal pain. BMs regular.
- GU: No dysuria or hematuria.
- Skin: No new rash currently. The pyrexia/rash episode has resolved. No photosensitivity reactions.
- MSK: No arthralgias or myalgias currently.
- Neuro: No headaches, no focal deficits.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. No hyperglycemia symptoms.
- Ocular: No blurry vision, no serous retinopathy symptoms (patient aware to report).

---

PHYSICAL EXAM
Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 68, Temp 37.1C, SpO2 98% RA, Wt 64 kg (stable)
ECOG PS: 0

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Good affect.
Skin: No palpable subcutaneous nodules over trunk, flanks, extremities (previously palpable nodules now completely resolved to palpation). No new pigmented lesions of concern on survey. No rash.
Head/Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.
Axillae: Bilateral axillary lymph nodes are not palpable today (previously enlarged).
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly appreciated on palpation today. No splenomegaly. Normal active bowel sounds.
Groin: No inguinal adenopathy palpable bilaterally (previously with bilateral inguinal nodes).
Extremities: No edema. No skin nodules on extremities.
Neurological: Grossly intact. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal deficits.

---

LAB RESULTS
Labs drawn today, results reviewed:
- CBC: WBC 5.8, Hgb 12.6, Plt 224 -- within normal limits
- CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 0.82, LDH 187 (within normal limits, was elevated at baseline) -- normalized and remains normal
- LFTs: AST 22, ALT 28, Alk Phos 74, T.bili 0.6 -- all normal
- Glucose: 94
- TSH: 2.1 -- normal (monitoring given dab/tram)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (reviewed today):
Hepatic lesions markedly reduced -- multiple lesions either barely visible or substantially smaller compared with baseline; the previously dominant right lobe lesion measuring 3.7 cm at staging is now approximately 1.0-1.1 cm. Several smaller lesions appear nearly resolved. Overall hepatic burden dramatically decreased, estimated roughly 70% reduction in visible disease. No new hepatic lesions. The bilateral inguinal and axillary lymph nodes have reduced substantially in size, many now in normal size range. Subcutaneous nodules not identified (resolved). Pulmonary micronodules stable in size and number, indeterminate/likely benign by morphology. No new sites of disease.

Brain MRI: Not repeated at this visit; last MRI negative at staging, no neurological symptoms, plan to repeat in ~3 months given M1c disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E+ melanoma, currently with impressive partial response to first-line Tafinlar/Mekinist (dabra 150 mg BID / Mekinist 2 mg daily).

Response assessment: This is a truly excellent response. LDH has normalized and has remained normal for the past 6+ weeks. Hepatic disease burden down dramatically. Lymph node disease markedly decreased. Subcutaneous nodules have completely resolved clinically. Patient feels well and is ECOG 0. We discussed how fortunate this response is, and I emphasized to her that this is exactly the kind of early deep response we hope for with targeted therapy.

Duration of response counseling: Had a detailed conversation with Ms. Castellano today about expectations going forward. I explained that unfortunately, while BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations like Tafinlar/Mekinist produce high response rates in BRAF-mutated melanoma, acquired resistance is nearly universal. The median duration of response with dab/tram is approximately 9-12 months in clinical trial data, though there is a meaningful tail of patients who do better. Given we are now at 3 months, it is entirely possible she could have 6-9 more months or longer of durable disease control -- but we need to be realistic that at some point, resistance pathways will likely emerge (RAS mutations, MEK amplification, alternative bypass signaling, etc.). She understood and appreciated the candid conversation. She asked thoughtful questions.

Future therapy planning: We discussed that when/if progression occurs, her next line of therapy would be immunotherapy -- specifically combination checkpoint inhibition with ipi (Yervoy) and nivo (Opdivo), or nivo monotherapy, depending on her clinical status at that time. I noted that her NGS showed TMB of 18.4 mut/Mb which is in the intermediate-high range and supports potential benefit from immunotherapy. The melanoma field has debated optimal sequencing of targeted therapy vs. immunotherapy upfront; for her, given the high disease burden and hepatic involvement requiring rapid disease control, we proceeded with targeted therapy first, which was the right call. We will transition to ipi/nivo if and when progression occurs. She is aware of the toxicity profile of combination checkpoint blockade and we briefly reviewed immune-related adverse events (irAEs).

Dose: Given she has been tolerating dab 150 mg BID well after retitration following the pyrexia episode, I am comfortable continuing and formally re-escalating to full-dose 150 mg BID (she is already on this per prior visit documentation). Tram 2 mg daily continuing.

Ongoing monitoring: Continue standard dab/tram toxicity monitoring. LFTs, CMP, CBC, LDH each visit. TSH monitoring every 2-3 months. Blood pressure monitoring (no HTN to date). Patient reminded to report fevers promptly, given pyrexia is a known and recurring concern. Also reminded to report any visual symptoms, skin rashes, or hyperglycemia symptoms.

2. Pulmonary micronodules -- stable, indeterminate, no intervention needed. Continue to follow on CT.

3. Health maintenance and supportive care -- Patient encouraged to use rigorous sun protection given melanoma history and southwestern US sun exposure (relevant to her landscape architecture work outdoors). SPF 50+, UV-protective clothing, hat use. She is aware.

Return to clinic: 3 months for restaging CT C/A/P and brain MRI. Sooner PRN for toxicity or new symptoms.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, more than half of which was counseling and care coordination.

[Electronically signed]
[Attending Oncologist, MD]
Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program

CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known stage IV melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, on targeted therapy (Tafinlar/tram). Restaging evaluation. Comparison made to prior CT abdomen/pelvis dated approximately 3 months ago.

---

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of IV iohexol contrast. Oral contrast was administered. Images acquired in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformations generated and reviewed.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs: No new pulmonary nodules identified. Scattered sub-5mm nodules in the right lower lobe, unchanged and felt to be indeterminate/likely benign given stability. No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Airways patent to the subsegmental level.
Mediastinum/Hila: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Cardiomediastinal silhouette unremarkable. No pericardial effusion.
Bony thorax: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: Multiple hepatic lesions again identified, overall appearing stable to minimally enlarged compared to the 3-month scan. The largest lesion, in hepatic segment VII, measures approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension — previously measured at 1.1 cm. The remaining lesions are scattered throughout both lobes, the majority measuring less than 1 cm, and are largely similar in size to prior. There is no new hepatic lesion identified. Enhancement pattern remains heterogeneous, without frank necrosis. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.
Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal lesion.
Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation.
Adrenal glands: No abnormality identified bilaterally.
Kidneys: Normal enhancement bilaterally. No hydronephrosis.
Gastrointestinal tract: No obstruction. No bowel wall thickening.
Peritoneum/Ascites: No ascites. No obvious peritoneal nodularity.

PELVIS:
Right inguinal region: A right inguinal lymph node measures 1.1 cm in short axis — previously 0.8 cm. This represents a slight increase in size compared to prior, though still within a range where clinical correlation is warranted. Left inguinal nodes unremarkable.
Pelvic organs: Uterus and adnexa appear within normal limits. No pelvic free fluid.
Bladder: Unremarkable.

SOFT TISSUE / SUBCUTANEOUS:
No discrete subcutaneous nodules identified. The previously noted subcutaneous soft tissue deposits are no longer visualized, consistent with continued resolution.

SKELETON:
No new suspicious osseous lesions. Previously noted incidental finding of mild degenerative changes at the lower lumbar spine, stable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Hepatic metastatic disease: The majority of hepatic lesions appear stable compared to the 3-month study. The dominant segment VII lesion is minimally enlarged at 1.4 cm (previously 1.1 cm). Overall, the prior dramatic reduction in hepatic burden appears to have plateaued, with the current scan suggesting early stabilization rather than continued regression.

2. Right inguinal lymphadenopathy: Slight interval increase in the right inguinal node (1.1 cm vs. 0.8 cm). Difficult to characterize definitively — could represent early nodal re-involvement versus reactive change. Warrants attention on next scan.

3. Subcutaneous disease: Not identified on this examination, in keeping with the prior near-complete resolution.

4. No new intrathoracic or intraperitoneal sites of disease.

Overall, the dramatic response seen through the 3-month timepoint appears to have stabilized. The degree of response is less pronounced compared to that earlier assessment, with modest changes in the dominant hepatic lesion and one inguinal node. LDH reported separately by the ordering team.

Signed: [Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging]

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known stage IV melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutated, on targeted therapy (Tafinlar/Mekinist). Restaging evaluation.

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Multidetector CT acquired with intravenous contrast in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal reformats generated.
MRI brain: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain performed at 3T with and without gadolinium contrast, including axial T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI, and post-contrast T1 sequences with thin-slice gadolinium-enhanced imaging.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain approximately 6 months prior.

---

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs: No new pulmonary nodules or masses identified. No pleural effusions. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. Mild dependent atelectasis at the lung bases bilaterally.
Mediastinum and hila: No new mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size within normal limits. No pericardial effusion.
Chest wall and soft tissues: Two new small subcutaneous nodules are identified along the right lateral thigh/upper leg (partially included on inferior pelvis images), measuring approximately 0.7 cm and 0.5 cm in greatest dimension. These were not present on prior imaging and are new from the previous study.
Axilla: There is new right axillary lymphadenopathy with a cluster of enlarged nodes, the largest measuring approximately 1.6 cm in short axis. These were not present on prior imaging.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is moderately enlarged. There has been substantial interval change compared to the prior scan. Previously, the hepatic lesions had shown marked reduction in size and number on the 3-month follow-up study. The current examination demonstrates definite interval increase in the size and number of hepatic metastatic deposits. The dominant lesion in the right lobe has enlarged and now measures approximately 4.2 cm in greatest dimension (previously measured approximately 3.1 cm). Additional known lesions throughout the right and left hepatic lobes are similarly enlarged compared to the most recent prior. Furthermore, there are at least three new hypodense hepatic lesions not previously identified, distributed throughout both lobes, the largest of which measures approximately 1.8 cm. Overall hepatic burden is substantially increased compared to the prior study.
Biliary: No biliary ductal dilation. Gallbladder appears unremarkable.
Spleen: Splenomegaly, stable in appearance. No focal splenic lesions.
Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilation or peripancreatic stranding.
Kidneys and adrenal glands: Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and enhancement pattern. No new renal lesions. Adrenal glands appear within normal limits bilaterally.
Aorta and vasculature: No aneurysmal change. No evidence of venous thrombosis on the images available.
Bowel: Bowel loops are non-distended. No pneumatosis or free air. No obstruction.

PELVIS:

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Impression: Ms. Castellano has unequivocal progression on Tafinlar/Mekinist after approximately 8 months of treatment. This is consistent with acquired resistance, which is expected in the majority of BRAF-mutant melanoma patients on targeted therapy within the first year. The progression pattern is multi-compartmental — hepatic, subcutaneous, nodal, and now CNS — with an elevated and rising LDH, all of which underscores the urgency of pivoting therapy. Her performance status remains excellent at ECOG 1.

This case was discussed at the multidisciplinary melanoma/CNS tumor board earlier this week. The board reviewed her genomic profile (BRAF V600E, CDKN2A del, TP53, PTEN loss, TMB 18.4), imaging, and performance status. Board members included medical oncology, radiation oncology (neurosurgery), neuroradiology, and surgical oncology. Consensus plan as follows:

1. **CNS Management — Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS):**
   The two brain metastases (R frontal 0.8 cm, L cerebellar 0.6 cm) are amenable to SRS given their small size, discrete margins, and limited number. The radiation oncology team will see Ms. Castellano for SRS planning. Whole brain radiation therapy was discussed but not recommended given the limited number of lesions and desire to preserve neurocognitive function, particularly in the context of anticipated systemic immunotherapy. Target dose discussed is 20 Gy in single fraction to each lesion (exact dosimetry per rad onc). SRS is recommended prior to initiating systemic immunotherapy to secure CNS disease control and mitigate risk of immune-mediated CNS inflammation from combination checkpoint blockade.

2. **Discontinue Targeted Therapy:**
   Tafinlar and Mekinist are to be discontinued today given confirmed acquired resistance. There is no role for re-challenge in this setting.

3. **Initiate Combination Immunotherapy — Yervoy + Opdivo:**
   Following completion of SRS (target: approximately 2-3 weeks), the plan is to initiate ipilimumab (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (the melanoma-dosed ipi/nivo regimen per CheckMate 067). Her TMB of 18.4 mut/Mb is in the elevated range and is supportive of immunotherapy benefit, though for melanoma this is not the primary driver of the decision. Her PTEN loss and TP53 status were noted but are not exclusionary. She does not have known MSI-H. Rationale for combination over single-agent nivo as second-line: improved response rates and OS data in melanoma, acceptable in ECOG 1 patient, CNS disease to be addressed by SRS prior to initiation. Risks and benefits reviewed in detail with the patient (see counseling below).

4. **Counseling:**
   Extensive counseling provided regarding the ipi/nivo combination, including the spectrum of immune-related adverse events (irAEs): autoimmune colitis (most common grade 3+), hepatitis, pneumonitis, hypophysitis, thyroid dysfunction, adrenal insufficiency, nephritis, dermatitis, and rarer events. Emphasized the importance of early reporting of diarrhea, abdominal pain, jaundice, new respiratory symptoms, or severe headache. Written patient education materials provided. Patient verbalized understanding and is in agreement with the plan. She is motivated and has strong social support.

5. **Labs prior to ipi/nivo initiation:**
   Baseline CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, free T4, cortisol, ACTH, LH/FSH, testosterone (if applicable), urinalysis, hepatitis B and C serologies to be ordered today.

6. **Steroid card / irAE wallet card** to be issued at time of first infusion.

7. **Supportive care:**
   Nutritional support referral placed given decreased appetite and mild weight loss. Social work referral offered; patient declined for now but will reconsider.

8. **Follow-up:**
   Radiation oncology SRS consult scheduled. Return to medical oncology approximately 3-4 weeks following SRS (i.e., approximately 5-6 weeks from today) for C1 Yervoy/Opdivo infusion and assessment. Patient instructed to call or present to ED immediately for any neurologic symptoms, severe diarrhea (>4 stools/day over baseline), or other concerning irAE symptoms.

Ms. Castellano is a 56-year-old female landscape architect with a history of stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma, originally diagnosed approximately 24 months ago. She had an early partial response at four weeks. This was complicated by febrile pyrexia, which was managed conservatively. Restaging at three months confirmed partial response with ongoing shrinkage; at six months, disease was stable. At approximately eight months, however, she had progression with new brain metastases identified on MRI.

At that point, she was referred to radiation oncology and underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to the brain lesions. She was then switched to ipilimumab plus nivolumab (ipi/nivo, Opdivo/Yervoy). After cycle 1 she was seen in follow-up with concerns for GI toxicity; she was hospitalized for grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, managed with high-dose corticosteroids and ultimately with infliximab. She recovered fully and ipilimumab was discontinued. She continued on Opdivo (nivolumab) maintenance monotherapy thereafter.

At 13 months she was responding on Opdivo. She developed hypothyroidism, consistent with immune-related thyroiditis, and was started on levothyroxine, which has kept her thyroid function stable. Restaging imaging at 15 months and again at 20 months showed progressive deepening of response -- the 20-month scan was notable for deep partial response with marked shrinkage of hepatic and nodal disease and stable brain. The case was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board attended by medical oncology, neurooncology, radiation oncology, and radiology. The board discussed at length the rationale for treatment pause at this juncture given: (1) cumulative immunotherapy duration now reaching approximately two years total from initiation of ipi/nivo; (2) near-complete systemic response with residual lesions of uncertain viability; (3) brain metastases stable and well-controlled following SRS with no new lesions on serial imaging; (4) patient's clear expressed preference to explore stopping therapy. ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This case demonstrates a genomic profile highly characteristic of cutaneous
melanoma arising in the context of UV-associated mutagenesis and aggressive
biologic behavior.

BRAF p.V600E (Tier I — Actionable):
The BRAF p.V600E substitution is the most common activating mutation in cutaneous
melanoma, present in approximately 50% of cases. This mutation results in
constitutive activation of the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway and is a well-validated
predictive biomarker for response to BRAF-directed targeted therapy. VAF of 48%
is consistent with a heterozygous mutation in a tumor of estimated 55% cellularity,
suggesting this is a clonal, truncal event. FDA-approved combination regimens
targeting BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma are available (see Therapeutic Implications).

CDKN2A / CDKN2B Homozygous Deletions (Tier II):
Biallelic loss of CDKN2A (encoding p16/INK4A and p14/ARF) and co-deletion of the
adjacent CDKN2B locus (encoding p15/INK4B) at chromosome 9p21.3 is among the most
frequent copy number events in melanoma. This simultaneously abrogates the Rb and
p53 tumor suppressor pathways (p16/Rb axis and p14ARF/MDM2/p53 axis), contributing
to unrestricted cell cycle progression. Co-deletion with TP53 mutation (see below)
represents dual impairment of p53 pathway regulation.

TP53 p.R248W (Tier II):
Arginine 248 is one of the most frequently mutated codons in TP53 across human
cancers. The p.R248W substitution is a well-characterized gain-of-function mutation
that not only abolishes wild-type tumor suppressor activity but confers oncogenic
gain-of-function properties including enhanced invasiveness and altered transcriptional
program. VAF of 41% suggests a clonal, early somatic event.

PTEN p.R130* (Tier II — Partial Loss):
The PTEN p.R130* nonsense mutation results in a truncated, non-functional protein
product, relieving inhibition of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. Sub-clonal
VAF of 22% suggests this may represent a later, secondary event in tumor evolution
or admixed clonal architecture. PTEN loss in melanoma has been associated with
reduced response to BRAF-targeted agents and with immune evasion phenotypes.

TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (Tier II):
The TERT promoter hotspot mutation at position c.-124 (also referred to as C228T)
creates a de novo ETS transcription factor binding site, resulting in upregulated
telomerase reverse transcriptase expression and telomere maintenance — a critical
hallmark of cellular immortalization. This mutation is present in the majority of
cutaneous melanomas and at high VAF (55%), consistent with a clonal event, likely
present in the founding clone.

MAP2K1 (MEK1) Amplification (Tier II):
Six-copy amplification of MAP2K1, encoding MEK1 — the direct downstream effector
of BRAF — represents a potential intrinsic or acquired resistance mechanism to
BRAF inhibition. In the context of BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma treated with
BRAF inhibitor monotherapy, MEK1 amplification can drive bypass signaling.
This finding underscores the importance of using combination BRAF+MEK inhibition,
which is standard of care for BRAF V600E-mutant disease.

RAC1 p.P29S (Tier III):
The RAC1 p.P29S mutation is a recurrent UV-signature mutation found in approximately
5-9% of cutaneous melanomas. It results in constitutive activation of RAC1 GTPase
and has been associated with reduced sensitivity to BRAF inhibitors in pre-clinical
models. Its relatively low VAF (12%) may reflect sub-clonal presence or tumor
heterogeneity. Clinical significance in combination therapy settings remains under
investigation; no approved targeted therapy directly addresses this alteration.

RB1 LOH (Tier III):
Loss of heterozygosity at RB1 without a detectable second-hit mutation by this
assay. In the context of concurrent CDKN2A/B deletion (which upstream regulates Rb
phosphorylation), functional cell cycle checkpoint impairment at the G1/S transition
can be inferred. Full biallelic RB1 inactivation cannot be confirmed without
immunochemical protein expression analysis.

Absence of NRAS mutation: NRAS and BRAF mutations are largely mutually exclusive in
melanoma, consistent with the identified BRAF V600E. The absence of concurrent NRAS
mutation is expected and relevant in confirming eligibility for BRAF-directed therapy.

TMB-High (18.4 mut/Mb): Elevated TMB in this melanoma is consistent with
UV-induced mutagenesis (C>T transitions at dipyrimidine sites — UV signature SBS7a/7b)
and may be associated with enhanced neoantigen burden. TMB-high status may predict
benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition, though in melanoma the relationship between
TMB and immunotherapy response is less clear than in some other histologies.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Tier I — FDA-Approved Indication (same tumor type):
  * BRAF p.V600E: Eligible for BRAF + MEK inhibitor combination therapy.
    FDA-approved options for BRAF V600E-mutant unresectable/metastatic melanoma
    include (not an exhaustive list):
      - Tafinlar (Tafinlar) + Mekinist (Mekinist)
      - vem (Zelboraf) + Cobimetinib (Cotellic)
      - enco (Braftovi) + Mektovi (Mektovi)
    NOTE: Given MAP2K1 amplification, combination BRAF+MEK inhibition is particularly
    indicated; BRAF inhibitor monotherapy is not recommended.

  * TMB-High (18.4 mut/Mb): Per FDA approval, pembro (Keytruda) has
    tumor-agnostic approval for TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors with prior therapy.
    Note: immune checkpoint inhibitor options are also independently approved for
    melanoma regardless of TMB status.

Tier II — Investigational / Emerging Evidence:
  * PTEN loss: PI3K/AKT pathway inhibition under investigation in melanoma (multiple
    clinical trials); no approved agent specifically for PTEN-loss melanoma.
  * RAC1 p.P29S: No approved targeted therapy; investigational interest only.

Not Detected / Negative Actionable:
  * NRAS mutation absent — confirms BRAF V600E-directed therapy is appropriate
  * KIT mutation absent — imatinib/targeted KIT therapy not applicable
  * NTRK fusion absent — larotrectinib/entrectinib not applicable

A second lesion in segment IVb, previously 2.4 cm, now measures approximately 0.8 cm and appears hypoattenuating on portal venous phase, suggesting treatment effect. Several additional smaller foci previously noted in segments II, III, and VIII are no longer confidently identified on this examination, though this may be partly related to image noise and technique. No new hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.

Spleen: Normal in size. No focal lesions.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear unremarkable. No discrete nodules.

Kidneys/Ureters: Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal mass or hydronephrosis. Small bilateral renal cysts, unchanged and benign in appearance.

Gastrointestinal Tract: Stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening or obstruction. No free air or free fluid.

Retroperitoneum/Mesentery: No enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes. No ascites.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: Dramatic interval decrease in bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. Previously noted enlarged bilateral inguinal nodes, largest on the right at approximately 2.8 cm short axis, are now markedly reduced — the right-sided node now measures approximately 0.9 cm. Left inguinal nodes similarly diminished. No new pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic Organs: Uterus and adnexa appear unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

Bladder: Unremarkable.

Osseous Structures: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions identified in the visualized skeleton.

Soft Tissues: Subcutaneous nodules previously identified along the flank, right upper back, and left posterior thigh are no longer visualized on the current study. No new soft tissue nodules identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval improvement in hepatic metastatic disease compared to the prior study approximately 3 months ago. The dominant lesion has decreased from 3.7 cm to 1.1 cm, and the majority of previously seen hepatic lesions are either markedly decreased or no longer visualized. Findings are consistent with substantial treatment response.

2. Markedly reduced bilateral inguinal and axillary lymphadenopathy, with nodes now near or below pathologic size threshold.

3. Previously identified subcutaneous nodules are no longer visualized.

4. Bilateral pulmonary micronodules stable and felt to be likely benign given unchanged morphology and distribution over serial imaging; continued surveillance appropriate per clinical discretion.

5. No new sites of disease identified on this examination.

Overall, imaging findings demonstrate a dramatic and widespread response to therapy across multiple sites of disease compared to the baseline study.
